Timothy Review - Empires & Puzzles Hero Strategy Guide
Timothy brings boosted health and a scaling attack buff to the Springvale family, but his self-defense down ailment is a controversial drawback. We analyze if his cute factor outweighs his risks.
Timothy Review: Is This Little Piggy Worth Going to Market For?
Timothy: The Brave Little Piglet with a Big Heart (and a Big Drawback)

Timothy is a 5-star Fire (Red) hero from the Springvale (Easter) family, released back in 2024. As a Rogue class healer, he wanders into battle with a knapsack full of carrots and a dream of adventure. His primary role is keeping your team alive with boosted health while ramping up their offensive capabilities. However, like most of his Springvale cousins, his power comes with a price—a self-inflicted ailment that can leave him vulnerable.

Visually, Timothy is arguably one of the most adorable heroes in the game. He’s a wide-eyed, smiling piglet dressed in a traveler's outfit, complete with a scarf and a bindle (that stick with a cloth bag). The art style is bright, cheerful, and completely at odds with the violent nature of dragon-slaying and empire-building. It’s hard to stay mad at him for dying when he looks this happy just to be included.

When Timothy was released in 2024, his stats were respectable, boasting high health to compensate for his self-defense debuff. However, looking at him through the lens of early 2026, he has been severely power-crept. Modern heroes from late 2025 (like the Titan Hunters or Super Elementals) are sporting stats in the realm of 1300+ Attack and Defense and 2200+ Health. Timothy's ~999 Power and sub-1000 Attack/Defense make him feel quite fragile by modern standards, essentially operating with the stat block of a modern 4-star hero.
Timothy's special skill tries to be a hybrid of survival and offense:

The mechanic of the attack buff is interesting but slow. It requires your other heroes to fire
him to ramp up the damage. This forces a specific firing order (Timothy first) and often requires a board full of mana to maximize the benefit within the short 3-turn window.

560 Boosted Health is fixed. Unlike percentage-based healers, this number doesn't scale with his troops or stats (except for healing bonus troops). In the era of 3000+ HP heroes, a 560 heal is roughly an 18-20% heal, which is on the lower side compared to modern percentage healers who can restore 45-50%.
To reach the maximum +70% attack, you need 5 other special skills to fire within 3 turns. In a standard 5-hero team, even if
fires immediately after him, you only get +40% additional (total +60%) for the last hero. You need mana generation or multi-casting to hit that +70% cap.
Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities
The Springvale family is known for simple bonuses and risky trade-offs.
The Attack bonus is nice, but fairly standard. It helps a bit with tile damage.

Upon release, Timothy was viewed as a "budget" option—a 5-star healer accessible via Epic Hero Tokens. He was never top-tier but served as a critical Red healer for players lacking premium options like Hathor or Garnet.

The game has moved fast. His lack of passives, average speed, and "low" fixed healing number have pushed him out of competitive play. The self-defense down, once a quirky trade-off, is now a death sentence against modern snipers who can hit for 1500+ damage effortlessly.

The community reaction to Timothy was... mixed, to put it politely.
"How did they come up with Mariol who boosts 700 health at fast... and then this guy comes here 560 boosted health at average speed... He is severely underpowered."
"This little piggy went to market... straight to Soul Exchange."
"I just found a use for Timothy - Owl Tower - boosted health is always helpful... almost to the top of impossible without even changing him."
"Garbage in beta and after release. Really sad that they ignored all feedback on this hero."

Timothy is a tragedy of timing. Released with an "old school" Springvale drawback in an era of "all upside" heroes, he feels like a relic despite not being that old. He is undeniably cute, and his boosted health is useful for newer rosters, but for serious competitive play, this piggy unfortunately stays home.
